messages.text: On the second day in the desert, the little prince sat near the engine while I worked. He never asked why metal mattered more than breakfast.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: If a sheep eats small bushes, will it also eat flowers?

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: A sheep eats whatever it finds. Why does that question carry such danger?
He did not answer. Instead, he described a planet so tiny that a chair could be moved a few steps to watch another sunset.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: My planet has three volcanoes. Two are active, and one may be sleeping, but I clean all three.
His routine sounded strange, yet he spoke of it like any careful housekeeper. Each morning he would inspect the surface after breakfast.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Good plants and bad plants look the same when they are young. The bad ones must be pulled by the root.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: What happens if you miss one?

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Then a baobab grows. Its root cracks everything. A small planet cannot carry that burden.
I understood then why my sheep had mattered. A sheep could eat the dangerous shoots before they became a disaster.
He asked for a muzzle too, because even useful sheep can cause harm. I promised one, then lost myself in the repair again.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: You speak like the adult people who count stars and forget flowers.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I am trying to stay alive in the desert. That is also a serious matter.
He looked hurt, and the narrow space between us filled with silence. I had answered a child with the impatience of an adult.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: If someone loves a flower on one planet, it is good to look at the sky and know that flower is there.
His voice shook. The desert wind moved around us, and suddenly the broken engine seemed less urgent than his hidden flower.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I will draw the muzzle. I should have listened to the request better.
He forgave me without saying the word. Then he returned to his small planet in memory, to volcanoes, sunsets, and dangerous roots.
That night I learned that a routine can be a form of love, and that a tiny burden may outweigh an adult machine.
Above us, the stars looked like sparks from another engine, each one perhaps carrying a planet too small for careless eyes.
He explained that small problems must be handled while they are still small. A careless morning could become a disaster no evening labor could repair.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: A baobab is not dangerous when it is tiny. That is why danger must be noticed before it looks important.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: So the sheep is not a toy. It is part of your routine and part of your defense.
He nodded and watched me sharpen my pencil. I promised to draw the baobabs so children on Earth would understand the warning.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Adults need pictures. They believe numbers, but sometimes they need pictures more than numbers.
That remark wounded my pride and healed it at the same time. I had once been a child with pictures; perhaps I could be useful again.
To help him, I made the baobab drawing bold and almost frightening. Children would understand that a root can begin as a thread and end as ruin.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I will draw the danger large, even if adults say the picture is too dramatic.
He described sunsets with the same care he gave to weeds. On a tiny planet, beauty and danger both arrive close enough to touch.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: If you wait one minute and move the chair, evening can happen again.
